title: Captive Microbiomes
text:
Short blurb about what the research goals are
While working with Dr. Aspen Reese at UCSD, I discovered a deep interest in what happens to the microbiomes of animals when taken into human care. Microbiome composition is deeply tied to environment, and the environments of animals in captive spaces are fundamentally different from those of wild animals, in a way that could have implications for animal health. A casual curiosity turned into a full literature review, published in 2021 in the journal Animal Microbiome.
Talk about peromyscus
Talk about Whooping cranes

title: Jess Diaz
text:
Hello!
I am a 5th year PhD student in the Kohl Lab at the University of Pittsburgh.
As a researcher, I am particularly interested in learning about how specifically different environmental elements and/or enrichment of animal enclosures may influence the health and wellness of animals living under human care. My research is very application-oriented with an eye towards conservation.
I am also interested in academic teaching, and have experience teaching both lecture-based and lab-based courses in subjects such as ecology and biochemistry.

Diaz J, Redford KH, Reese AT. 2023. Captive and urban environments are associated with distinct gut microbiota in deer mice (Peromyscus maniculatus). Biology Letters 19(3), 20220547. Open Access Link
Diaz J, Reese AT. 2021. Possibilities and limits for using the gut microbiome to improve captive animal health. Animal microbiome 3(1), 89. Open Access Link



Supporting author publications

Kuthyar S, Diaz J, Avalos-Villatoro F, Maltecca C, Tiezzi F, Dunn RR, Reese AT. 2023. Domestication shapes the pig gut microbiome and immune traits from the scale of lineage to population. Journal of evolutionary biology 36(12), 1695-1711. Open Access Link



Additional research support

Acknowledged contribution to study design in Williams et al. 2026. Captivity drives multi-generational shifts in the gut microbiome that mirror changing animal fitness. mBio 17(2). Open Access Link
